seo_title: Completion vs Optimality in Policy Gradient Methods
meta_description: Researchers identify two orthogonal failure modes for policy-gradient methods: completion and optimality, proposing a decomposition to separate them.
body_html:
The researchers identify two orthogonal failure modes for policy-gradient methods: completion and optimality. Completion refers to reaching the terminal horizon, while optimality involves matching the dynamic-programming reference given completion.

Failure Modes in Policy Gradient MethodsCompletion: Reaching the terminal horizon rather than exiting via an implicit terminal constraintOptimality: Matching the dynamic-programming reference given completionPPO with Linear Soft PenaltyThe penalty's equilibrium drives the dominant-activity share to zero, while action-space restriction combined with horizon access achieves completion but leaves an optimality gap ( rac14) that we trace to first-phase greedy commitment at the damage origin.Testable Predictions and EvaluationPredictionResultHorizon-invariance predictionMet at three of four tested horizons, with the exception at H = 15 consistent with the H^* boundary (H^* otin [6, 14] under

the NBA parameters)Action-space restriction combined with horizon access achieves completionTrueCompletion rate reduced by granting horizon access aloneTrue

source.title: Completion vs Optimality: Policy Gradient in Long-Horizon Cumulative-Damage Problems
source.summary: Researchers have identified two failure modes for policy-gradient methods in long-horizon cumulative-damage problems: completion (reaching the terminal horizon) and optimality (matching a dynamic-programming reference given completion). They propose a decomposition to separate these modes and test four predictions, which are met qualitatively in two environments with different domains, horizons, and activity sets. The results show that granting horizon access alone reduces completion rates, while action-space restriction combined with horizon access achieves completion but leaves an optimality gap.
